items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation:
I’ve got that nagging feeling again...
Shpak has a tape recorder,
and the ambassador has a medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: Core catchphrase is rendered in a reasonably elevated way (“nagging feeling”), but it loses the mock-heroic/theatrical flavor of «терзают смутные сомнения» (more like “vague doubts torment/haunt me”). More importantly, the second clause is mis-scoped: in context, it’s not possession (“has”) but a parallel of losses/connected thefts (each had/was associated with an item that is now missing). Using “has” weakens the detective-like inference and contradicts the reference note that they no longer have the items. A tighter, elliptical structure would better preserve the comedic callback rhythm.

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation:
Hello, Tsar—pleased to meet you.
Tsar—pleased to meet you, hello.

Tsar—pleased to meet you.
Hello, Tsar—pleased to meet you!
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: The translation preserves the key comic mechanism: repetitive, banal politeness and the absurd insistence on “Tsar” as a social-introduction label. It’s not identical to the reference (it omits the explicit “I’m the Tsar”), but the effect—drunken, era-clashing etiquette loop—still lands. Punctuation with em dashes is fine for subtitle rhythm. Minor loss: without “I’m the Tsar,” the self-introduction aspect is slightly less explicit, but still inferable.

items.translation: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Identical to the reference and fully preserves the sudden authoritarian, power-drunk register shift.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me be, old woman—I’m in sorrow.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: Meaning and high-register melancholy are preserved. “Leave me be” is slightly more modern/colloquial than the reference’s more formal “Leave me,” but the archaic-dramatic contrast still works, and “I’m in sorrow” keeps the elevated tone. Subtitle dash is acceptable.

items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: The core gag (technology read as demonic entombment) is present, but the English is clipped in a way that obscures the intended passive meaning (“They’ve walled me in”). “Bricked up” reads like an adjective/condition and can carry unintended slang associations in modern English; it also drops the subject/agent that the reference flags as important (“demons” as the doers). This weakens both clarity and the mock-biblical melodrama.

items.translation: That’s what the life-giving cross can do!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: Accurate and idiomatic. It keeps the religious framing and the triumphant attribution of a mechanical event to faith. Slightly less mock-solemn than “Behold the power…”, but still elevated enough to carry the joke in an atheist-modern setting.

items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: Meaning is broadly conveyed (a command to dance), but it loses the specific performative/MC-style formulaic feel of «Танцуют все!»—a set phrase closer to “Everyone’s dancing!” / “All dance!” / “Everybody on the floor!” The reference aims to capture the announcer-like opening of the dance floor, which is part of the comic anachronism. “Everybody dance!” is understandable but flatter and more generic.

items.translation: What a perfect time to drop in!
items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: Captures the opportunistic satisfaction and comic timing well. “Perfect time to drop in” is natural and conveys the self-congratulatory tone. Slight nuance: the Russian is a punchy catchphrase; “Now, this was a good call!” is snappier, but the provided translation still works and remains funny in context.

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation:
Black caviar, red caviar...
and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: Identical to the reference and correctly avoids calling the eggplant spread “caviar,” preserving the Soviet-status inversion joke.

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation:
is gone: three tape recorders,
three movie cameras,

three domestic cigarette cases,
a suede jacket—three.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: The escalating inventory and the final “three” are preserved, including the comedic afterthought on the suede jacket count. “Domestic cigarette cases” matches the intended boastful specificity. Minor rhythm loss: the reference’s ellipses mimic Shpak’s breathy, self-important listing; the translation is slightly more streamlined, but still performs the gag.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation:
And they’ll cure you!

And they’ll cure you too!

And they’ll cure me!
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: Faithful and effective: keeps the calm, sing-song fatalism and the shared-absurdity punchline. Exclamation marks add a bit more energy than the reference’s trailing ellipses; this slightly shifts tone from resigned to emphatic, but does not break the joke.

summary.interpretation: 2.18 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.44 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
